turns.content: I thought I could just pass a regular function into my `move` closure and it would capture the context automatically, so why does my code still panic with an unimplemented trait error when I try to use it?

That's a common assumption. The issue is that while closures can capture variables by reference or move them, they don't guarantee that the captured data implements specific traits like `Debug` just because you passed them in. If your closure tries to call methods on types that aren't automatically implemented for the trait bound, you'll get an error. You need to ensure every type you pass into a generic higher-order function explicitly implements the required trait, or use `dyn Trait` if you want dynamic dispatch instead of static coherence checks.
Okay, so I tried adding `impl Debug` manually but that didn't work either. Does this mean Rust closures are completely incompatible with generics unless everything is perfectly typed?

Not quite. You don't need to manually add trait implementations for every type inside a closure if you're using the right approach. Instead of writing custom `impl` blocks, you can leverage Rust's orphan rules and generic bounds on your higher-order function itself. For example, define your function with a generic parameter that requires `trait Trait`, then pass types that naturally satisfy those bounds without needing extra boilerplate. This keeps your API flexible while avoiding the manual trait impls you were trying to force.
I see, but what if I need to compose two closures where one returns something different than the other expects? Does generic dispatch handle the type conversion automatically?

Composition works well when your types are homogeneous or when you're using `dyn Trait` for dynamic dispatch. If you have a chain like `closure1(closure2(input))`, Rust will infer the return type of the inner closure and match it against the expected input of the outer one. However, if the types don't align perfectly, generic static dispatch might fail, so switching to dyn trait dispatch in your API can make composition more flexible by allowing any type that implements the trait at runtime.
That sounds promising, but I'm worried about performance since dyn trait dispatch involves a vtable lookup. Is it really worth the overhead for my game logic?

You're right to be concerned about overhead. Static generic dispatch is generally preferred for performance-critical code like game loops because it avoids the indirection of vtables. Dyn trait dispatch should only be used when you genuinely need to accept arbitrary types at runtime, perhaps in a plugin system or configuration handler where flexibility outweighs raw speed. For typical game logic, stick with generic bounds and explicit trait implementations rather than relying on dynamic dispatch.
Got it, but I still have this weird error about orphan rules when trying to extend my engine's internal types. Does Rust allow me to add new traits to existing library crates?

The orphan rule prevents you from adding traits to foreign types unless the trait itself comes from that same crate or a dependency. If your engine defines a `trait GameEvent`, then you can implement it for any of its internal types. But if you're trying to implement a trait from another library on your own types, Rust will reject it. The solution is either define the trait within your project's scope or refactor your API to use dyn trait dispatch so the trait origin doesn't matter as long as the concrete type implements it at runtime.
Ah, that clarifies things! So for my game engine, I should just keep all event traits internal and avoid importing external traits into my closure signatures?

Exactly. By keeping trait definitions scoped to your project, you can safely use generics with those traits in your closures without hitting orphan errors. This also makes it easier to compose events across different game systems since they all share the same trait interface. You can then pass these closures around freely as long as every involved type implements the internal trait, avoiding the need for dynamic dispatch unless you specifically want that flexibility.

I'm trying to refactor our legacy logging system but it's currently using static function pointers everywhere. I want to make the API more flexible so we can inject different log levels without changing the core logic, but my initial thought is that we can just use a generic trait bound on the logger interface and pass any implementation of Logger into the function.

That approach sounds promising for static dispatch, but there's a catch with generics in Rust. If you define your logging function to take a parameter implementing some Trait like Logger, it will only work with concrete types at compile time because generic parameters are monomorphized. You won't be able to swap the logger dynamically without recompiling or using unsafe code to bypass type checking. For runtime flexibility where different loggers can be injected, you need to move away from generics and use dyn dispatch instead.
So I should avoid generic parameters on my function signature entirely? Can I just use a Box<dyn Trait> somewhere in the middle of my code?

You're getting closer but there's a subtle detail about memory safety. Using Box<dyn Trait> allows you to hold different implementations at runtime, which is exactly what you want for plugging in new loggers without recompilation. However, since dyn Trait requires an object slice or smart pointer, your function signature needs to accept something like &dyn Logger or Box<dyn Logger>. This means the logger type cannot be known at compile time anymore because it's part of the runtime polymorphism.
Okay so I'm accepting a reference to a trait object. But here's my concern - what happens if someone passes an old-style function pointer instead? Will that even work with dyn dispatch?

You've hit on a key distinction between static and dynamic dispatch. Function pointers are for generic/static dispatch scenarios, while dyn Trait objects require the type to be an object implemented by some trait like Logger. They're fundamentally different mechanisms in Rust's type system. A function pointer cannot be coerced into a Box<dyn Logger> because one is a concrete function address while the other represents a polymorphic runtime value.

That makes sense, so I should definitely avoid using function pointers here and stick with trait objects instead. But what if we want to compose multiple closures together? Like chaining loggers or filtering messages?

Composing closures is actually one of the best use cases for higher-order functions in Rust. You can write a pipeline function that takes a closure as its argument, like fn pipe<F>(f: F) where F extends some trait like FnMut. Then you can chain multiple operations together by passing different closures into that single function. This lets your team build complex logging pipelines without writing repetitive boilerplate code.

I see how that helps with composition now. But I'm worried about the complexity level - is there a way to keep it simple enough for our engineering team who aren't as comfortable with advanced Rust concepts?

You can definitely simplify this by wrapping the higher-order function in a helper crate or using macros if your team prefers that approach over raw closures. However, even without macros, you can provide a default closure implementation and let users override it only when necessary. This way, most code paths remain simple while still allowing flexibility for edge cases where advanced features are needed.
That's a good middle ground idea. But one last thing - what about the performance implications? Are closures slower than regular functions when we're doing high-throughput logging?

Performance-wise, closures are generally comparable to regular functions in Rust because they compile down to standard function calls or inline code depending on the optimizer's context. The main overhead comes from indirection through trait objects if you're using dyn dispatch, which adds a small virtual call table lookup. For high-throughput logging, this is usually negligible unless you're doing millions of operations per second in a tight loop.

Okay, so the trade-off is between compile-time flexibility with generics versus runtime flexibility with trait objects, and closures help us bridge that gap nicely. I think we should proceed with rewriting our logger interface using dyn dispatch and higher-order functions.

That sounds like a solid plan for your team's next iteration. By moving from static function pointers to dynamic trait object-based APIs, you'll gain the flexibility to swap loggers without recompilation while keeping performance acceptable through careful closure design. Just remember to test thoroughly with different logger implementations before deploying.
